calculator.title: Waiting Time Penalty Calculator
calculator.subtitle: Calculate potential penalties for late final wage payments
calculator.laborCodeText: If an employer willfully fails to pay wages due at termination, the employee's wages continue as a penalty from the due date at the same rate until paid or for 30 days, whichever is less.
calculator.dailyWageCalc: Daily wage = hourly rate x 8 hours
calculator.maxPenalty: Maximum penalty is 30 days (capped by law)
calculator.resultPlaceholder: Enter wage information and days late to calculate the penalty
calculator.additionalNote: This is in ADDITION to the unpaid wages still owed. Total liability = unpaid wages + waiting time penalty.
calculator.example1Text:
Final pay 10 days late
Daily wage: $160
Penalty: $1,600
calculator.example2Text:
Final pay 5 days late
Daily wage: $200
Penalty: $1,000
calculator.example3Text:
Final pay 30 days late
Daily wage: $164.38
Penalty: $4,931.51
calculator.disclaimerText: This calculator provides estimates for informational purposes only. Actual penalties may vary based on specific circumstances, good faith disputes, and other factors. The "willfulness" requirement means penalties may not apply if the delay was due to a genuine dispute over wages owed. Always consult with an employment attorney for specific situations.
calculator.ctaText: ToolTime Pro includes automated final pay tracking, compliance alerts, and the full ToolTime Shield suite.

classification.title: Worker Classification Flowchart
classification.subtitle: Determine if your worker is an employee or independent contractor under California AB5
classification.abcTestDesc: Under AB5, a worker is presumed to be an employee unless the hiring entity can prove ALL THREE conditions of the ABC test are met.
classification.conditionAQuestion: Is the worker free from the control and direction of the hiring entity in performing the work, both under the contract and in fact?
classification.conditionAHelp: Consider: Do you set their schedule? Provide tools/equipment? Supervise their work methods? Tell them how to do the job?
classification.conditionBQuestion: Does the worker perform work that is outside the usual course of the hiring entity's business?
classification.conditionBHelp: Consider: Is this work part of your main business service? Would customers see this as part of what your company does?
classification.conditionCQuestion: Is the worker customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business of the same nature as the work performed?
classification.conditionCHelp: Consider: Do they have their own business? Other clients? Business cards? LLC or contractor license?
classification.employeeWarning: Based on your answers, this worker should likely be classified as an EMPLOYEE. Misclassification can result in:
classification.employeePenalty2: Unpaid overtime and missed meal/rest breaks
classification.contractorNote: Based on your answers, this worker MAY qualify as an independent contractor. However:
classification.contractorWarning1: This is not legal advice - consult an attorney
classification.disclaimer: This tool provides general information only and does not constitute legal advice. Worker classification is complex and fact-specific. Always consult with an employment attorney for your specific situation.
classification.ctaText: ToolTime Pro includes worker classification tracking, AB5 compliance alerts, and the full ToolTime Shield suite.

checklist.title: AB5 Compliance Checklist
checklist.subtitle: Comprehensive checklist to ensure your business complies with California AB5 requirements

items.class1: Conducted ABC Test analysis for all workers
items.class2: Documented reasoning for each classification decision
items.class3: Reviewed any industry-specific exemptions that may apply
items.class4: Consulted with employment attorney on borderline cases
items.doc1: Written independent contractor agreements in place
items.doc3: Proof of contractor's independent business (license, insurance, other clients)
items.doc4: Clear scope of work documented for each project
items.pay1: Contractors paid by project/invoice, not hourly
items.pay2: No reimbursement for tools, equipment, or expenses
items.ongoing1: Annual review of all contractor relationships
items.ongoing2: Training for managers on classification rules
items.ongoing3: Process to evaluate new hires for proper classification
items.ongoing4: Monitoring for changes in working relationship

checklist.warningText: If you answer "No" to any ABC Test condition for a worker, that worker should likely be classified as an employee, not an independent contractor.
checklist.disclaimer: This checklist is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Consult with an employment attorney for specific guidance.
checklist.ctaText: ToolTime Pro includes automated compliance tracking, deadline reminders, and the full ToolTime Shield suite.

finalWage.title: Final Wage Rules Guide
finalWage.subtitle: Know exactly when final pay is due based on termination type in California
finalWage.overviewText: California has strict deadlines for paying final wages. The timing depends on whether the employee quit or was terminated, and whether notice was given.

involuntary.title: Involuntary Termination (Fired/Laid Off)
involuntary.description: Final wages must be paid immediately at the time of termination.
involuntary.details: This includes all earned wages, accrued vacation/PTO, and any other compensation owed.

voluntaryNoNotice.title: Voluntary Quit (No Notice)
voluntaryNoNotice.description: Final wages must be paid within 72 hours of the employee's last day.
voluntaryNoNotice.details: The 72 hours is calendar hours, not business hours. If an employee quits on Friday, payment is due by Monday.

voluntaryWithNotice.title: Voluntary Quit (72+ Hours Notice)
voluntaryWithNotice.description: If employee gives at least 72 hours notice, final wages are due on their last day.
voluntaryWithNotice.details: The notice must be at least 72 hours in advance. If they give less notice, the 72-hour rule applies.

seasonal.title: Seasonal Workers (Film/Agriculture)
seasonal.description: Special rules may apply for seasonal workers in certain industries.
seasonal.details: Film industry: 24 hours. Agriculture: varies. Check specific industry requirements.

finalWage.includeItem1: All hours worked through the final day
finalWage.includeItem5: Final paycheck in the employee's usual form
finalWage.penaltiesText: Under Labor Code 203, if final wages are willfully withheld, the employer owes waiting time penalties:
finalWage.penaltyCalc: Daily wage rate × days late (up to 30 days maximum)
finalWage.penaltyExample: Example: A worker earning $25/hour ($200/day) who is paid 15 days late could receive $3,000 in penalties.
finalWage.tipItem1: Have a final paycheck ready BEFORE a planned termination
finalWage.tipItem2: Keep a petty cash fund or checkbook for immediate payments
finalWage.tipItem3: Document the time and method of final wage payment
finalWage.tipItem4: Get a signed receipt when delivering the final check
finalWage.tipItem5: Include a detailed pay stub with the final payment
finalWage.disclaimer: This guide provides general information about California law and does not constitute legal advice. Consult with an employment attorney for specific situations.
finalWage.ctaText: ToolTime Pro automatically calculates final pay amounts and reminds you of deadlines based on termination type.
